It was a script for Eastman Kodak, right? And what made it so great was this story I told you. You've heard me say this before where I used a comparison. I told a story, right? Remember if you've gotten to the, to the bonus modules, I'd say a hell back. I left out two key points in the training for scripting. I left out this, A, that we want to link all goods and services to trustworthy figures. And I explained how to do that in the bonus module. And B, I also left out the idea of telling the story, not just giving dry facts and figures, right? What made the Kodak script so awesome was that it was an idea that, you know, that the reason Kodak, the stock had dropped was they'd been sued by Polaroid for patent infringement and the stock had dropped from 40, it was actually 85 down to like 38 at the low, right? And basically the thesis was that every time a major company had gotten sued like that, the stock had dropped like a rock. And I gave three examples. I told the story. I said, I said, you know, Kodak, here was the pitch. It was like, you know, you know, right now, name the stock Eastman Kodak, you probably know it, Blue Chip Company all the way, right? It's a big film, blah, blah, blah, blah. Okay. Right now the stock's trading at $45. Okay. We think this is going to $95 in the next 30 days. Let me tell you exactly why. That's the opening. What would I say next? Well, the company based on earnings is, no, no, no, no, no. I'd say remember when that plant exploded in Boat Pal India back, remember this is 88. So five years ago, it was 1983 Boat Pal India, all right? And the stock of union carbide. I'm sorry, let me see if you get exactly how it was. Right? I said, so I said, let me tell you exactly why. Remember five years back that plant exploded in Boat Pal India, union carbide and 13,000 Indians were killed. Remember that? And they say, oh yeah. Well, on the day that plant exploded, the following day it opened, the stock of union carbide dropped from 92 down to $12 a share. Mass litigation. Three years later, the day the litigation was settled, that stock roared back to $67. No major winner. A few years later, US Steel was sued by Nipon Steel for patent infringement. What happened to that price? It's the stock. The stock went from $29 a year down to four bucks. They that litigation was settled and started to wrap back to 32. Another huge winner. Texaco was sued by Penn's Oil again for patent infringement. On the day Texaco was sued, the stock was from 67 down to 11. They that litigation, two years later, the stock ran back to $94 a massive winner. So Jim, you know, the obvious key to making money in situations like this, position yourself now before litigation. Now our legal analyst senses that Kodak is going to settle with Polaroid virtually any day and it's a half billion dollars, they have that money set aside in their balance sheet right now. Let me ask you a question, Mr. Jones. Where do you think the stock is going after they settle with Polaroid? All the institutions comes roaring back at the Kodak stocks. Where's the stock going to go? And say, well, it's going up. I say, exactly. That's called selling through comparison and metaphor. I didn't say right now Kodak stock is trading at 42. They earn $4 a share. The entire consumer products industry trades 25 times a year. That's a hundred bucks. I didn't, I made that case too, but on my second, on my first loop. I started off by framing this big picture case that every time these major companies get sued like this, the stock just gets destroyed. And the reason for that is there are certain restrictive covenants in the corporate charters of major institutions that bar them from getting involved in companies facing major litigation. They can't buy the stock. So once litigation settles, these institutions comes roaring back into a stock, they go flying up. Makes sense so far. The guy says, yes, exactly. Now the key to making money in a situation like this is to position yourself now before you read about it in the Wall Street Journal. Because when you're talking about it there, it's simply too late. The entire consumer products industry trades 25 times earnings through the math, 25 times 400 dollars. Right now the stock is 42. Take Kodak, break it up, sell it off in small chunks.
